cot_prefix: Okay, I need to figure out the answer to this question. The question is about a passage regarding the copyright of "Happy Birthday to You" and asks what the history experts' statements prove. The options are (A) to (D). Let me read through the passage again to make sure I understand it. The main points are: - Jennifer Nelson had to pay $1,500 to use "Happy Birthday to You" in her documentary, which is about the history of the song. The money went to Warner Music Group, who claim they own the copyright since 1988. - The original song was written by the Hill sisters as "Good Morning to All" in 1893. People then started using the "Happy Birthday" lyrics with the same tune. - History experts say there's no record of who wrote the "Happy Birthday" lyrics and that the public was singing it long before it was printed and owned by a company. - Nelson's lawyers argue this history shows the song belongs to the public, so Warner Music shouldn't charge fees. They want a court to make Warner return fees collected in the past four years. - Warner/Chappell has made about $2 million a year from licensing fees. The question is about what the history experts' statement proves regarding the "Happy Birthday" song. Let me look at the relevant part of the passage again: "A number of history experts say that there is no record of who actually wrote the 'Happy Birthday to You' lyrics. Historians also say there is no way to know when the general public began singing the 'Happy Birthday' song, but they believe it was being sung by the public long before it was printed and owned by a company." So the experts are saying two main things: 1. No record exists about who wrote the lyrics. 2. The public was singing it long before a company printed and owned it. Nelson's lawyers use this to argue that the song belongs to the public, hence Warner Music Group shouldn't have the copyright and shouldn't charge fees. The question is asking what the experts' statements prove regarding the song. Now let's look at the answer choices: (A) does not belong to Warner Music Group (B) has more than 200 years' history (C) was initially owned by another company (D) has always been very popular Let's evaluate each option: (A) does not belong to Warner Music Group: The experts' statements suggest the song was in the public domain (people were using it before a company owned it). This would support the argument that Warner Music Group does not have the copyright, which is what Nelson's lawyers are arguing. So this could be the correct answer. (B) has more than 200 years' history: The original song was written in 1893, so it's about 130 years old (as of 2023), not 200. The experts don't mention the song's age in terms of centuries. So this is incorrect. (C) was initially owned by another company: The experts don't say anything about another company owning it initially. The history is about the song being in the public domain and then being claimed by Warner Music Group in 1988. There's no mention of another company owning it before that. So this is not supported by the passage. (D) has always been very popular: The experts say it was being sung by the public long before it was printed and owned by a company, which could imply popularity, but the passage doesn't directly state it "has always been very popular." The experts are focusing on the fact that it was used publicly long before a company owned it, not necessarily on its popularity.
